content.problemStatement:
**This is the easy version of the problem. Here you need to construct any valid permutation PPP. In the hard version, you need to count the number of valid permutations.**
**This version is worth 100100100 points Division 111 and unscoreable in other Divisions.**

Stack had a permutation PPP of the integers [0,N−1][0, N - 1][0,N−1].

Based on that, he calculated an array AAA of length NNN as follows:


Ai=mex(P1,P2,...,Pi)A_i = \text{mex}(P_1, P_2, ..., P_i)Ai​=mex(P1​,P2​,...,Pi​) **OR** Ai=mex(Pi,Pi+1,...,PN)A_i = \text{mex}(P_i, P_{i + 1}, ..., P_N)Ai​=mex(Pi​,Pi+1​,...,PN​)
Now, Stack forgot the original permutation PPP.

In this version, he needs to **find any valid permutation** PPP that could have been the original permutation, given the array AAA. It may be possible that none exist, in which case print −1-1−1.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line contains NNN - the size of the permutation.
The second line contains NNN elements - A1,A2,...,ANA_1, A_2, ..., A_NA1​,A2​,...,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the following:



if a valid permutation exists, NNN integers - P1,P2,....,PNP_1, P_2, ...., P_NP1​,P2​,....,PN​. (0≤Pi<N,Pi≠Pj0 \le P_i < N, P_i \ne P_j0≤Pi​<N,Pi​=Pj​ for i≠ji \ne ji=j)
−1-1−1 otherwise.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 2 : We show the construction for the array AAA:

Choose the 2nd2^{nd}2nd option for A1A_1A1​, i.e. A1=mex(P1,P2,P3)=3A_1 = \text{mex}(P_1, P_2, P_3) = 3A1​=mex(P1​,P2​,P3​)=3.
Choose the 2nd2^{nd}2nd option for A2A_2A2​, i.e. A2=mex(P2,P3)=0A_2 = \text{mex}(P_2, P_3) = 0A2​=mex(P2​,P3​)=0.
Choose the 1st1^{st}1st option for A3A_3A3​, i.e. A3=mex(P1,P2,P3)=3A_3 = \text{mex}(P_1, P_2, P_3) = 3A3​=mex(P1​,P2​,P3​)=3.
